score_scale: Integer 1-5 per criterion (5 best), five equally weighted criteria; mean is the arithmetic average of the five scores.

candidates.label: A
candidates.rationale: Mechanism (a shadow follows you, so it is bad at hiding) is purely conceptual and carries identically into fr/es/ja with no wordplay dependency. All four renderings, including the Japanese, read naturally and idiomatically.
defects: Comic payoff is a gentle riddle rather than a true groaner; groan factor is modest.
candidates.recommendation: Strong contender; top-tier for multilingual robustness.

candidates.label: B
candidates.rationale: Classic English name-pun ('I'm hungry' reparsed as identity). The mechanism depends on the English copula 'I am'; it collapses in French/Spanish, which express hunger with 'have' (j'ai faim / tengo hambre), and there is no name-parse at all in Japanese.
defects: 'Salut Faim' and 'Hola Hambre' are calques with no comic trigger in the source phrasing.
Japanese 'はじめましてお腹すいた' is unnatural and carries no pun.
English-only wordplay, explicitly disfavored by the goal.
candidates.recommendation: Reject for multilingual use; lands only in English.

candidates.label: C
candidates.rationale: Quintessential call-and-response dad-joke format with a universal physical pun: two walls literally meet at a corner, and 'meet at the corner' is a rendezvous idiom in every target language. Strong groan while staying language-agnostic; Japanese '角で会おう' is crisp and natural.
defects: Spanish 'esquina' denotes an outer corner where walls form an inner 'rincón'; a minor semantic seam, not a comic break.
candidates.recommendation: Winner; best blend of genuine dad-joke groan and universal mechanism.

candidates.label: D
candidates.rationale: Echo-repeats-everything is a fully conceptual mechanism that transfers cleanly to all four languages. Payoff is self-evidently literal and the Japanese reads naturally.
defects: Gentle observational humor; groan is mild rather than a true dad-joke wince.
candidates.recommendation: Strong contender; excellent multilingual equivalence, slightly soft punchline.

candidates.label: E
candidates.rationale: Same name-pun template as B but with 'tired', which uses the copula in en/fr/es ('I am tired'), so the parse partly survives in French/Spanish. However treating an adjective as a proper name is unnatural, and Japanese '疲れた' as a name does not work at all.
defects: Adjective-as-name ('Salut Fatigué', 'Hola Cansado') is grammatically odd and reads as a calque.
Japanese 'はじめまして疲れた' fails to produce the pun.
Depends on copula grammar rather than a universal comic mechanism.
candidates.recommendation: Revise or reject; better than B but still breaks in Japanese.

candidates.label: F
candidates.rationale: The double sense of 'looked surprised' (felt surprise vs. physically appeared surprised because the eyebrows are drawn high) is a visual/semantic pun that survives in all four languages; the Japanese renders naturally.
defects: Longest setup of the set; two-clause narrative reduces brevity.
Slightly more inference required than a pure call-and-response joke.
candidates.recommendation: Solid multilingual joke; ranks just behind the tighter formats.

panel_note: All six candidates pass the completeness gate (en/fr/es/ja present, Japanese in Japanese script), so all are eligible. C, A, and D tie at mean 4.6; the tie is broken toward the goal's dual emphasis on genuine dad-joke groan plus a universal mechanism. C wins the tie for combining the strongest recognizable dad-joke format and groan with a physical (non-linguistic) pun; A edges D for a marginally more playful hide-and-seek setup, though both offer near-perfect cross-language equivalence with softer punchlines. F ranks fourth on a universal visual pun with a longer setup. E and B are the English-copula name-puns that fail to carry into Japanese and read as calques in French/Spanish; E scores above B because 'I am tired' preserves the copula parse in en/fr/es whereas 'have hunger' in B breaks it. This is the key grammatical-calque trap the brief warns about: B and E preserve literal meaning across languages but lose the comic trigger.
